draft.introduction:
The Sumerian King List opens with one of the most consequential theological claims in ancient literature: “When kingship descended from heaven, kingship was in Eridu.” With this single line, the boundary between divine and human authority is erased. Kingship is not a human invention; it is a divine institution that literally fell from the sky. The kings who wield it are not merely powerful men—they are conduits of a heavenly force.

The text survives in multiple copies, the most complete being the Weld-Blundell Prism (c. 1800 BCE) now in the Ashmolean Museum. The list enumerates kings from before the Flood through historical dynasties, blending myth and chronicle without distinction. The antediluvian kings reign for tens of thousands of years; after the Flood, the reigns shorten to human scale. The theological implication is that the Flood did not merely destroy life—it diminished the divine element in kingship itself, pushing humanity further from its heavenly origin.

translation.text: When kingship descended from heaven, kingship was in Eridu. In Eridu, Alulim became king; he ruled for 28,800 years. Alalgar ruled for 36,000 years. Two kings ruled for 64,800 years. Then Eridu fell, and the kingship was taken to Bad-tibira.
translation.original_text: nam-lugal an-ta e₂-de₃-a-ba nam-lugal-la eriduᵏᴵ-ga a-lu-lim lugal mu 28800 i₃-ak

translation.text: In Bad-tibira, En-men-lu-ana ruled for 43,200 years. En-men-gal-ana ruled for 28,800 years. The divine Dumuzi, the shepherd, ruled for 36,000 years. Three kings ruled for 108,000 years. Then Bad-tibira fell, and the kingship was taken to Larak.
translation.original_text: bad₃-tibiraᵏᴵ en-men-lu-an-na mu 43200 i₃-ak en-men-gal-an-na mu 28800 i₃-ak dDumu-zi sipa mu 36000 i₃-ak

translation.text: Then the Flood swept over. After the Flood had swept over, and the kingship had descended from heaven again, the kingship was in Kish.
translation.original_text: a-ma-ru ba-ur₃-ra-ta a-ma-ru ba-ur₃-ra-ta nam-lugal an-ta e₂-de₃-a-ba nam-lugal-la kišᵏᴵ-a

translator_notes.note: The phrase “namship descended from heaven” (nam-lugal an-ta ed-de-a-ba) uses the verb ed, “to descend,” the same verb used for divine beings coming down to earth. Kingship is treated as a me—one of the fundamental divine powers or cosmic principles that the gods hold and occasionally grant to humanity. The city of Eridu, the oldest city in Sumerian tradition, is associated with the god Enki (Ea), the god of wisdom and the same deity who features in the Adapa myth. The convergence is not accidental: the first city, the first king, and the first sage all emerge from the same place where heaven touched earth.

The Flood marks the great rupture. Before it, kings reign for fantastical periods—28,800 years, 43,200 years—numbers that are multiples of 3,600 (the Sumerian shar, a fundamental unit of large numbers). After the Flood, reign lengths gradually approach historical plausibility. The theological implication: the Flood did not just destroy life; it reduced the divine charge in human kingship. The antediluvian kings were semi-divine; the postdiluvian kings are merely human with a divine mandate. This is a narrative of progressive de-divinisation—the mirror image of the theosis tradition’s claim that humanity can move in the opposite direction.
